title: Theory of Anderson localization on the hyperbolic plane
authors: Alexander Altland, Tobias Micklitz, Devasheesh Sharma, Maksimilian Usoltcev, Carolin Wille
abstract: The two-dimensional hyperbolic plane, $\mathbb{H}^2$, is an unusual system in that dimensionality changes with scale: locally two-dimensional and planar at short distances, but effectively infinite-dimensional at large scales, it provides an interesting paradigm for the study of (quantum) phase transitions, notably the disorder-driven Anderson transition. Generalizing previous work, which treated short and large distance scales separately, we develop a unified framework interpolating between the principles of low- and high-dimensional Anderson localization. As a main result, we derive a two-parameter flow in a plane spanned by scale-dependent curvature (setting the system's effective dimensionality) and conductivity, with an extended critical line separating metallic and insulating phases.
summary: This paper provides a complete theory of Anderson localization on a surface with negative curvature. It explains how the geometry of the hyperbolic plane causes a crossover from standard 2D localization to a high-dimensional regime where metallicity can be protected. By using both field theory and graph-based models, the authors derive a unified renormalization group flow that predicts a true phase transition between metallic and insulating states.
main_problem: Developing a unified theoretical framework for Anderson localization on the hyperbolic plane that interpolates between 2D-like behavior at short scales and infinite-dimensional behavior at large scales.
main_result: The derivation of a two-parameter renormalization group flow for conductivity and curvature, identifying a critical line (separatrix) that separates metallic and insulating phases.
method: A combination of a non-linear sigma-model using the supersymmetry formalism, renormalization group analysis, and lattice discretization using Poisson-Delaunay random graphs.
model_or_system: The two-dimensional hyperbolic plane (H2) with constant negative curvature, modeled via a Hamiltonian with a delta-correlated random potential and discretized using various graph structures like Bethe lattices and Poisson-Delaunay graphs.
key_observables: Scale-dependent conductivity (sigma), scale-dependent curvature (u), resistance (R), and critical exponents (nu and beta).
important_parameters: Disorder strength (W), curvature radius (L), conductivity (sigma), and the scaling variable (u).
main_assumptions: One-loop approximation in the renormalization group integration; use of a delta-correlated random potential; assumption that the Poisson-Delaunay discretization accurately captures the continuum spectrum in the high-density limit.
figures_summary: Figure 4 shows a generation of a cactus graph; Figure 5 is mentioned in the context of discretization/graph properties.
paper_structure: The paper begins with a continuum sigma-model to establish the 2D-like localization physics, moves to a lattice discretization (Bethe lattice and random graphs) to capture the high-dimensional transition, and concludes by deriving the coupled RG flow equations and the critical line.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: A number of diffusive Brownian motion processes simulated on H2. The dashed curves indicate geodesic circles of radii r = (1, 2, 5)L. Note the crossover from diffusive to ballistic behavior, as trajectories escape to infinity.

FIG. 3: Discretizations of the hyperbolic plane: (a) Pois- son–Delaunay random graph, (b) q = 4 Bethe lattice, (c) (p, q) = (3, 2) Husimi-cactus graph, (d) {p, q} = {7, 3} regu- lar polygon tessellation. Discussion, see text.

FIG. 2: Flow of the two parameters (σ, u) as a function of the RG length scale. A separatrix (red line) divides the pa- rameter plane into a metallic and an insulating regime. In the metallic regime (above the separatrix), the RG flow ter- minates (u(ℓ) ∼1) at finite, non-zero conductivity, while in the complementary, localizing, regime, the conductivity flows to zero on an RG length scale below the curvature scale and localization occurs before the effect of the curvature is felt.

Fig. 2 visualizes the flow (σ, u)(ℓ) traced out by the solutions above. Note the extreme efficiency of the hy- perbolic plane in protecting metallicity: for systems with weak to moderate disorder σ > 1, even a small curva- ture with large radius L ∼Λ−1 exp(2πσ) suffices to block strong localization. In this parameter domain, the cutoff u(ℓ) ∼1 below which loop interference becomes ineffec- tive is reached at finite residual values of the conduc- tivity. The boundary to the localized regime is marked by a separatrix, σsep(u) defined by the condition that σsep(u →1) ≲1, i.e. a conductivity suppressed down to small values at u ∼1. FIG. 4: Two cell generations of a cactus with q = 3 (p = 4)- gons meeting at each vertex.

FIG. 5: Spectra of different graph discretizations of a trun- cation of H2 at a finite radius R in units of curvature radius L, compared to the continuum theory (black) of the same ge- ometry with Dirichlet boundary conditions. The curves are scaled manually to a coinciding lowest mode value. (The lat- ter is finite, and determined by the effective curvature whose detailed comparison between different discretizations is not straightforward.) We notice strong deviations between the continuum and both Bethe, and Husimi cactus discretizations.
